item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] FURTHER TRIBUTES
item.description: Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS : if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here . REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

This week’s cartoon is pretty self-explanatory, as our hard-working president installs gold statues of himself on one of his golf courses and prepares to spend one billion taxpayer dollars on his ballroom. Trump has actually floated the idea of adding his head to Mount Rushmore, though according to a long article I read in the New York Times, this would be a geological impossibility. I just decided to up the ante, having him replace all four existing heads with his own, and in the process created one of the most cursed images I believe I have ever drawn. The only line that may confuse people is the word balloon in the bottom middle panel, in which I am deliberately referencing the revised signage on the Kennedy Center: “The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Center.” Which makes me cringe every time I read it. And in this version, Lincoln doesn’t even get his first name, which was also a deliberate choice. Also, the final panel was meant to evoke that thing in North Korea where everyone has the same haircut as the glorious leader, but I felt that specifically referencing it would be too heavy handed.

***

Flaming Hydra, the collective and writers and artists I was recently invited to join, had a MAD magazine week last week, spearheaded by Zach Rabiroff. When the fact of my teenage visit to the offices of MAD came up in the group chat, I was encouraged to write about it. I think it’s behind a paywall, but as subscribers to this newsletter, you get the bonus content. (Though I do encourage you to support the Hydra!) Big thanks to Zach, Maria Bustillos, and Joe McLeod for invaluable editing and fact-checking assistance.

MAD man

I grew up on MAD Magazine, and it had a profound impact on both my creative sensibilities and my general outlook on the world. Through the subversive satire of MAD, a whole generation of kids in the 1960s and 1970s learned to distrust advertising, politicians, and authority in general—all of which turned out to be lessons that would serve me well in life. And on a specifically personal note, it’s obvious that I was heavily influenced by the magazine’s text-heavy movie and TV parodies. Whenever someone suggests that my cartoons have “too many words,” I just want to say: have you ever seen an issue of the 20th century’s preeminent journal of satire and whimsical commentary, cleverly disguised as a comic magazine for children? 

MAD was published by the legendary William M. Gaines, a man seemingly born to the job. The American comic book itself had, arguably, been invented by his father, Max. In 1933, Gaines père was employed as a salesman at Eastern Color Printing when he took advantage of some unused printing presses to package newspaper comic strips into bound booklets. Funnies on Parade turned out to be an enormous hit, and Gaines went to found two companies of his own: All-American Publications (later merged into what became DC Comics), and then Educational Comics, or EC for short.


Funnies On Parade (1933) fair use image via Wikipedia

When Max was killed in a motorboat accident in 1947, his n’er-do-well son Bill took over the family business, and within a few years began publishing MAD in its original comic book format, along with a variety of suspense, science fiction, and horror titles—a body of work that would one day land him before a U.S. Senate subcommittee. There, he was interrogated by Tennessee Senator Estes Kefauver, who was less than appreciative of his contributions to American culture.

“This seems to be a man with a bloody axe holding a woman’s head up which has been severed from her body,” Kefauver famously said at one point. “Do you think that is in good taste?”

Gaines responded by defending his artist’s restraint and subtlety, noting that a different composition might have emphasized even more blood.

Within two years of the disastrous hearings, industry censorship and public opprobrium had led Gaines to shutter his comic book line—but not MAD, which had converted to a larger magazine format a year earlier.  The rest was history. 
 
***

How much did I love this magazine? When I was in my early teens, I’d visit my dad for the summer in Iowa, and I spent a significant portion of my days in the Rare Books room at the University of Iowa library, reading back issues and plugging nickels into a mid-1970s photocopier to make poor-quality, barely-legible Xerox copies of my favorite pieces. Older material from the magazine was also available in paperback anthologies (Certifiably MAD, Raving MAD, etc.), which I’d pored over at an even younger age, struggling to understand the numerous references that predated me, and, by extension, to make sense of the mysterious adult world they parodied. The MAD version of West Side Story was especially perplexing, moving the location east to the United Nations, and turning the rival street gangs into politicians from either side of the Iron Curtain. Since I had never seen the movie, nor had much idea who Khrushchev even was, it was like trying to decode a mysterious artifact that had somehow fallen through a hole in space and time—a key to secret knowledge I could sense, but not quite comprehend. 

There were dozens of movies I only knew through their MAD parodies. A while back, I spent a few weeks finally watching some of these, including Lawrence of Arabia—or, as I first knew it, “Flawrence of Arabia.” It was strangely familiar, like a friend I had only ever seen reflected in a funhouse mirror. And I became well-versed in secondhand cultural references, like the fact that the Ford Edsel had been a colossal failure, or that there had briefly been a television show about a man’s mother reincarnated as his car. When I started writing and drawing my own childhood cartoons a few years later—many of them multipage movie satires, drawn on lined notebook paper with a No. 2 pencil—they included punchlines referencing both the Edsel and My Mother the Car. Because I had read the jokes in MAD, and I knew they were funny, even if I didn’t fully understand why.

I could go on, but you get the idea. I was a fan, a devotee, and a student of MAD. I read, and re-read, and re-re-read Frank Jacobs’s 1972 biography, The MAD World of William M. Gaines, immersing myself over and over in the story of Gaines and his ostentatiously zany band of cartoonists (aka “the usual gang of idiots”). Looking back over that book recently, I realized that some of their wacky office pranks were actually just kind of cruel, and from my current vantage point, I was appalled that MAD’s artists did not retain any rights to their own work. Nonetheless, Gaines was the ringmaster of a circus that enchanted and captivated me, and he undeniably created something extraordinary in the process.

***
 
In my senior year of high school in Iowa, I applied for a U.N.-sponsored trip to the East Coast. I had moved around quite a bit as a kid, from Iowa to Florida to Georgia to Arkansas, and eventually back to Iowa; I was a child of divorce, and my mom always seemed to believe in the promise of the next horizon, the fresh start. Which was, in fairness, more of a possibility before the internet settled over us all like smog trapped under an inversion layer. I inherited her restless spirit, and was in a hurry to see more of the world. Within a couple of years I would drop out of college and move to New York City with barely any money in my pocket, and live in Williamsburg long before it was “Williamsburg.” But that’s an essay for a different day.
I was living with my dad at this point, and I saw a way to visit New York that he’d probably allow, despite his deeply small-town distrust of big cities. I submitted some sort of contest essay about the noble purpose of the United Nations, or the vital necessity of world peace, or whatever it was, I honestly have no idea. But it got me on the bus. We drove from Iowa to D.C. and got a tour of the Capitol from our Congressman, stopped in Philly to see the Liberty Bell, and then, finally, arrived in New York City.

We couldn’t have been greener, more clearly from the sticks, myself most of all. I got my first suit for that trip, and I use the term loosely: the salesman sold my father on a camel’s hair jacket and a pair of navy blue slacks, which made me look like a security guard in a mid-tier department store. But here’s the wild thing: somehow, apart from the various structured activities, the trip chaperones basically let a bunch of kids from Iowa run free in late-seventies New York City, unsupervised. And so of course the first thing I did was grab a couple of my buddies and make a beeline for the address I knew so well from the masthead of every issue of my favorite magazine: 485 MADison Avenue. 

How we even found our way there, wandering around an unfamiliar and overwhelming city, I don’t really know. But somehow we did, and, I guess, announced that we were teenagers from Iowa here for a visit, and… they invited us right in, which was apparently standard office policy whenever random kids showed up. Longtime MAD editor Nick Meglin gave us a tour of the offices: the stacks of flat files (what I wouldn’t give now to have had a half an hour just to poke around in that treasure trove); a wall covered in sketches from all the artists whose styles I knew so well—Jack Davis, Sergio Aragonés, Don Martin, Al Jaffee. 

And then a visit to Gaines’s office.



Photo: Dan Perkins

He was an avuncular man in his late fifties, with long grey hair swept back from his forehead and what I can only describe as a jolly beard. He wore a short-sleeved pinstriped shirt and thick black-framed glasses, the case for which was visible in his shirt pocket. According to his biography, he was compulsively neat and kept the items on his desk precisely arranged, but all I remember is a chaotic jumble: MAD zeppelins hanging from the ceiling, a giant paper mache King Kong head (designed by Aragonés) peering in through a window. 

Gaines greeted us from behind his desk, and then paused and gave me a piercing look and said, slowly and with great intensity, “Young man—you are clearly destined to become a modestly successful cartoonist with a relatively small, yet fiercely loyal, readership.” The lights flickered briefly and the windows rattled as a strange breeze whipped through the room, sending papers and artwork flying off his desk.

Okay, that didn’t really happen. Actually he chatted with us politely for a moment as I snapped a few photos with the Nikkormat I carried pretty much everywhere in those days, and then offered us coupons for something called THE NEW YORK EXPERIENCE, which Google tells me was a “long-running multi-screen extravaganza.” We didn’t use them, but I’ve had a lifetime of New York experiences since then, so I guess it all evens out. 

And that’s pretty much it. For a few short minutes, I stood in the presence of this absolute icon, more of a hero to me at that age than any musician or actor—not really a creator himself, but an enabler of creativity, and of cartooning, and satire, and much of what I hold dear in this world. This man who personified MAD Magazine at least as much as the gap-toothed mascot they’d appropriated from turn-of-the-century advertisements and old penny postcards. The moment still looms large in my memory, nearly half a century later: I made the pilgrimage to that shrine of an office, and I shook the hand of the man that made MAD.



Photo: Dan Perkins

 And that's it for another week! 

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list
The new Gunroad store, where you can purchase ebooks of TMW25 and OLNN!
Topataco store-- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more
Threadless store -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff
Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com
Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow
Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] LESSONS LEARNED
item.description: Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS : if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here . REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

This week’s cartoon refers back to an incident that occurred a full week ago, which is about a decade in Trump Time. After a gunman tried to get into the Correspondents’ Dinner ballroom, and was stopped by security before he got in, the Trump administration — never ones to waste a good opportunity — immediately pivoted to the need for Trump’s ballroom. And various Republicans started talking about using taxpayer money to pay for it, in direct contradiction of Trump’s oft-repeated promises that the whole thing would be paid for by private donors. This raises many questions. Is the WHCA, a private organization unaffiliated with the White House, supposed to let the president host their events moving forward? What better way to demonstrate the independence of the media! Of course, as far as I’m concerned, the whole thing was shameful to begin with, and should have been canceled anyway. As the last panel suggests, the media have no business celebrating freedom of speech with an administration devoted to the exact opposite. 

And while I am no fan of James Comey, the idea that an instragram pic of seashells spelling out “86 47” was intended as a call for assassination is absurd on the face of it. 86 is a food service term for something being out of inventory, and also slang for getting ejected from a bar due to unruly behavior. It's not a term for killing someone! In my experience, people who get kicked out of bars are not summarily executed! Like so many things in this administration, it is ludicrous on the face of it, because they are lying and they know that most people know they are lying, and the very act of it is an assertion of power.

Oh, and if you’re wondering why Patel is wearing that hat — here’s a picture of him from his podcaster days, hawking K$H merch. Extremely serious people we’ve got, running this government of ours.

Wish list thanks to St. Chris, Pam S., and this week's inevitable unintentionally-anonymous gifter!

And that's it for me this week!

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list
The new Gunroad store, where you can purchase ebooks of TMW25 and OLNN!
Topataco store-- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more
Threadless store -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff
Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com
Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow
Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social
Newsletter (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)
Paypal donations 
Tiny View link (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE BEST AND THE BRIGHTEST
item.description: Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS : if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here . REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

So, I made it to Iowa City and back, a whirlwind weekend trip sponsored by my local paper there, Little Village, and their publisher, my friend Jordan Sellengren. We had a comics panel featuring several local cartoonists and moderated by Kembrew McLeod, with whom I have previously spoken at length for an interview. Also in attendance were Paul Brennan, with whom I spoke by Zoom when my previously-scheduled event which was supposed to celebrate LV’s 25th anniversary had to be canceled due to the big East Coast blizzard last January; former FCC head and longtime Iowa City resident Nicholas Johnson; and a former art professor of mine, Mel Andringa. Mel says that he always remembered me from my feedback about guest speakers in class symposiums, which was apparently notably cranky for an 18 year old — “why are we even listening to this person?”, or something like that. I have no memory of this, but have to admit that it does sound like something I would say, especially as a young man untempered by a little more life experience. Anyway, it was great to meet and/or spend more time with everyone.

While the regional Cedar Rapids airport is actually closer to Iowa City, I know from long experience, when I used to fly in to visit my dad, that CR shuts down at the slightest hint of a raindrop, so I flew direct to Des Moines. My flight Friday evening, on one of those small regional jets, was delayed by bad weather — a string of tornadoes throughout the midwest, apparently — and while there were no stomach-churning altitude drops, there was plane-rattling turbulence most of the way there, the kind that makes you feel like you’re in an Indiana Jones movie and you’re not entirely sure the plane is going to hold together, so that was fun. And then my flight out was delayed by an hour due to a ground stop at LGA because of fog. If I’m not sleeping on an airport floor, and I make it where I’m going in one piece, then I figure I don’t have too much to complain about, but the weather on this trip was not my friend.

And fortunately the trip schedule was such that I was able to get back home in time to correct last week’s truly inexplicable typo — “revals” instead of “rivals”. Don’t ask me where *that* came from, but thanks to all who pointed it out — I’d rather have too many emails about a typo than none.

This week’s cartoon is a bit of a hodgepodge of various news stories from the last week or two. I realized too late that I missed my window of opportunity to do something in time for the White House Correspondent’s Dinner, with the media hosting the man who wants to throw them all in jail, and bravely protesting by wearing little first amendment pocket squares, god help us all. You probably know that Obama’s mild roast of Trump at the WHCD is Trump’s supervillain origin story, allegedly inspiring him to run for the presidency out of revenge. I actually attended one of these things myself, back when Clinton was president — media reformer James Fallows had been put in charge of US News and World Report by Mort Zuckerman, and brought me in to contribute a cartoon for a few months, until Zuckerman said “what the hell is this?” and made him drop me. As a consolation prize, Jim invited me to sit at the US News table. I saw Oliver North there, with whom -- fun fact -- I shared an editor at St. Martin's Press at the time, and Sharon Stone, who was dating the editor of the San Francisco Examiner, where I'd run for a number of years, and then somehow talked my way into the Vanity Fair afterparty where I chatted briefly with Matt Drudge. What can I say, it was the nineties. (A few years later, Drudge would steal a photo I took of Michael Moore arguing with Bill O'Reilly in a parking lot at the DNC in Boston, clumsily altering it by flipping it horizontally and changing the traffic lights so that they were green in all directions, but that's another story.)

(LAST MINUTE UPDATE: I had the WHCD on in the background last night when I looked up and saw that ... things were not normal. Trump reportedly wanted to come back out and give his speech after the shooter was apprehended, but the secret service wasn't on board with that. I am curious if he would have come out and given the same speech, in which he was planning to roast the media. He seemed momentarily subdued when he spoke to the press corps later in the evening, though of course with him that never lasts with him. And he did still manage to use the incident in order to promote his ballroom, where he said such a thing never would have happened, and I suppose he’s technically correct — security inside the perimeter of one of the most highly protected complexes on the planet is probably better than that of a hotel on the street. I was also reminded, watching the chaos unfold, that the year I went, someone at US News told me to be at the Hinckley Hilton at a certain time, and I spent a bunch of time trying to figure out what and where the Hinckley Hilton was, until someone finally told me it was a rather dark nickname commemorating the Reagan assassination attempt, not the official name of the hotel.)

The first panel of this cartoon reflects my own deeply held belief, borne of simple observation, that Trump’s staff works desperately to keep bad news away from him. The second panel presented a dilemma — basically everything else in this strip is based on an actual news story, and every panel could have the “this is true!” footnote, but I felt like this one particularly needed it. Dr. Oz told the story in a podcast that Trump believes that diet soda, because it kills grass when poured on the lawn, might also kill cancer. That seems like a story a lot of readers might have missed, and without the footnote, I think a lot of people would be left scratching their heads at that panel.

Pete Hegseth did just eliminate mandatory flu vaccinations for the troops, because why would troops residing in tightly packed quarters possibly need protection from highly communicable diseases. Hegseth has famously said that he doesn’t wash his hands after going to the bathroom because he doesn’t believe in germs. RFK is also said to reject germ theory, the 19th century foundation of modern medical science, in favor of the miasma theory, dating back to ancient Greece, that disease is caused by bad air. 

You have probably read that Kash Patel is suing the Atlantic, for its reporting on his partying and erratic behavior, including a morning after when he could not be roused, allegedly leading his underlings to request breaching equipment to get through his locked door. If this lawsuit actually proceeds to discovery, there may be popcorn shortages, because that’s going to be quite the show. I mean, we have all seen the photo of Kash Patel chugging a beer as he partied with the Olympic hockey team, while the search for Savannah Guthrie’s mother was in full swing back in the states. I threw in the Trump “who are you, again?” line because I suspect that his days in the administration may be numbered, though you can never really be sure — Trump seems to mainly fire women, after all.

Trump’s presidential daily briefing really does consist of a two-minute sizzle reel of things blowing up in Iran, according to news reports. And there does seem to be a lot of insider trading going on, both in the stock market and the prediction markets, conveniently timed around Trump’s various shifting pronouncements. 

It has been a busy week in Tomorrowland! Wish list thanks to Daniel N., and what may be a new record of three unintentionally anonymous gifters (no gift receipts in the packages), including the generous soul who sent the cookware set, for which I am most grateful. Bye-bye, cheap teflon cookware!

And so, until next time,

Dan

Wish list
The TT Tiny Store, featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.
Topataco store-- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more
Threadless store -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff
Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com
Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow
Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social
Newsletter (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)
Paypal donations 
Tiny View link (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] WHAT WILL HE SCREW UP TODAY
item.description: Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS : if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here . REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

Greetings, readers of the future! I’m writing these words on Thursday, to be queued up for weekend distribution. This is something I always struggle with, given that I usually queue up the newsletter shortly before it goes out, keeping an eye on the news the whole time to make sure you-know-who hasn’t done something that negates the previous week’s work somehow. But I had a curtailed work week due to upcoming weekend travel, which incidentally always screws up my writing process. Even though I generally try to have the cartoon wrapped up by Friday, part of being able to do that is knowing that I have the weekend as a buffer zone in case I run into a mental block or something happens in the news that necessitates a change to the cartoon. It’s pretty neurotic, I’m aware, but this is the brain I’ve got to work with and I do the best I can with it. It makes travel complicated; I haven’t been on a proper vacation since 2023. I’m hoping to switch that up this summer, even if it means running the occasional evergreen that, like this one, may end up feeling completely out of sync with the news cycle, depending on what history’s most special boy Donald J. Trump does on any given week. Of course, with rising travel costs and the general global uncertainty unleashed by history’s most special boy, well. We shall see if these best laid plans gang a-gley.

Anyway I decided to go with an evergreen from my drafts folder this week. Which gives you all that rarest of treats, a cartoon that does not even mention Donald Trump! I’ve had this one mostly ready to go for awhile, though once I pulled it out I had to keep poking and prodding at it, because that’s just how I roll. It took me awhile to decide which tech billionaires to use. Altman and Zuckerberg felt obligatory, but Musk could have been just as easily replaced with Bezos. (Any more than three of them would have crowded the panel too much.) And I try to keep my cartoons internally consistent, so I felt that while I was presenting them as a united front, I also needed to add the line about how they all hate each other — Musk and Altman, in particular, are currently feuding, and of course Musk and Zuckerberg once talked about having an MMA style fight, though Musk backed out of that. I was also aware that Sparky’s rundown of a.i. drawbacks was incomplete, and certain that I would be endlessly reminded of this throughout the week, so I added his line “and that’s just for starters.” A.i. proponents will undoubtedly let me know that ACTUALLY the technology has *many* uses while ignoring the secondary question of the cartoon — at what cost? This is all being shoved down our throats by a handful of billionaires who want to reshape the world in their image and don’t care much about consequences. Are the benefits they claim worth the economic and environmental havoc they generate? Is your chatbot pal worth wrecking the entire world? It reminds me of a novel that I read in high school, by Thomas M. Disch, called The Genocides, about an invasive alien plant species that colonizes Earth and eventually makes it unlivable. I feel like we’re watching the digital version of this happening in real time and I am not a fan. 

ANYWAY! I’m off soon on my whirlwind trip to my old stomping grounds, Iowa City, a make-up trip I promised last January when I had to cancel an appearance there due to the gigantic blizzard. I fly in late Friday and out Sunday morning, so I won’t have as much time there as I would have preferred. I wanted to stay an extra day but the flight schedules for Monday just didn’t work out. Maybe I’ll see/will have seen some of you there! As I say, this is a missive from the distant past of last Thursday. 

Wish list thanks to Michael B., Nova V., and the (probably unintentionally) anonymous gifter who sent the cooking pan (no gift receipt, once again). I’m not proud of this, but when I was first divorced I barely knew how to cook, and I left all the good cookware with my ex. It made sense at the time, our son was still in high school and she was going to be cooking for the two of them. One of her parting gifts on my way out the door was a set of really cheap, terrible cookware, the kind you might buy for a kid going off to college for the first time. Now it’s eight years later and I have become a normal human who cooks pretty regularly, and that stuff is in bad shape — it’s getting beat up, and it’s teflon, so it’s possibly even hazardous to my health at this point — and I’m starting to replace it with, you know, grown-up cookware. I’m 65 years old now, it feels like time! Now if only I could learn to dress like an adult! But you can go a long way with t-shirts and jeans when you are a cartoonist.

Okay, I think that’s it for this week. I appreciate you all, you’re literally the reason I am still able to do this work. Given that I have mostly outlived the altweekly industry that birthed my career, that’s no small thing.

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list
The TT Tiny Store, featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.
Topataco store-- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more
Threadless store -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff
Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com
Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow
Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social
Newsletter (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)
Paypal donations 
Tiny View link (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE VISIONARIES
item.description: Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS : if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here . REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

Greetings, readers of the future! I’m writing these words on Thursday, to be queued up for weekend distribution. This is something I always struggle with, given that I usually queue up the newsletter shortly before it goes out, keeping an eye on the news the whole time to make sure you-know-who hasn’t done something that negates the previous week’s work somehow. But I had a curtailed work week due to upcoming weekend travel, which incidentally always screws up my writing process. Even though I generally try to have the cartoon wrapped up by Friday, part of being able to do that is knowing that I have the weekend as a buffer zone in case I run into a mental block or something happens in the news that necessitates a change to the cartoon. It’s pretty neurotic, I’m aware, but this is the brain I’ve got to work with and I do the best I can with it. It makes travel complicated; I haven’t been on a proper vacation since 2023. I’m hoping to switch that up this summer, even if it means running the occasional evergreen that, like this one, may end up feeling completely out of sync with the news cycle, depending on what history’s most special boy Donald J. Trump does on any given week. Of course, with rising travel costs and the general global uncertainty unleashed by history’s most special boy, well. We shall see if these best laid plans gang a-gley.

Anyway I decided to go with an evergreen from my drafts folder this week. Which gives you all that rarest of treats, a cartoon that does not even mention Donald Trump! I’ve had this one mostly ready to go for awhile, though once I pulled it out I had to keep poking and prodding at it, because that’s just how I roll. It took me awhile to decide which tech billionaires to use. Altman and Zuckerberg felt obligatory, but Musk could have been just as easily replaced with Bezos. (Any more than three of them would have crowded the panel too much.) And I try to keep my cartoons internally consistent, so I felt that while I was presenting them as a united front, I also needed to add the line about how they all hate each other — Musk and Altman, in particular, are currently feuding, and of course Musk and Zuckerberg once talked about having an MMA style fight, though Musk backed out of that. I was also aware that Sparky’s rundown of a.i. drawbacks was incomplete, and certain that I would be endlessly reminded of this throughout the week, so I added his line “and that’s just for starters.” A.i. proponents will undoubtedly let me know that ACTUALLY the technology has *many* uses while ignoring the secondary question of the cartoon — at what cost? This is all being shoved down our throats by a handful of billionaires who want to reshape the world in their image and don’t care much about consequences. Are the benefits they claim worth the economic and environmental havoc they generate? Is your chatbot pal worth wrecking the entire world? It reminds me of a novel that I read in high school, by Thomas M. Disch, called The Genocides, about an invasive alien plant species that colonizes Earth and eventually makes it unlivable. I feel like we’re watching the digital version of this happening in real time and I am not a fan. 

ANYWAY! I’m off soon on my whirlwind trip to my old stomping grounds, Iowa City, a make-up trip I promised last January when I had to cancel an appearance there due to the gigantic blizzard. I fly in late Friday and out Sunday morning, so I won’t have as much time there as I would have preferred. I wanted to stay an extra day but the flight schedules for Monday just didn’t work out. Maybe I’ll see/will have seen some of you there! As I say, this is a missive from the distant past of last Thursday. 

Wish list thanks to Michael B., Nova V., and the (probably unintentionally) anonymous gifter who sent the cooking pan (no gift receipt, once again). I’m not proud of this, but when I was first divorced I barely knew how to cook, and I left all the good cookware with my ex. It made sense at the time, our son was still in high school and she was going to be cooking for the two of them. One of her parting gifts on my way out the door was a set of really cheap, terrible cookware, the kind you might buy for a kid going off to college for the first time. Now it’s eight years later and I have become a normal human who cooks pretty regularly, and that stuff is in bad shape — it’s getting beat up, and it’s teflon, so it’s possibly even hazardous to my health at this point — and I’m starting to replace it with, you know, grown-up cookware. I’m 65 years old now, it feels like time! Now if only I could learn to dress like an adult! But you can go a long way with t-shirts and jeans when you are a cartoonist.

Okay, I think that’s it for this week. I appreciate you all, you’re literally the reason I am still able to do this work. Given that I have mostly outlived the altweekly industry that birthed my career, that’s no small thing.

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list
The TT Tiny Store, featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.
Topataco store-- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more
Threadless store -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff
Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com
Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow
Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social
Newsletter (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)
Paypal donations 
Tiny View link (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] WHAT WILL HE SCREW UP TODAY
item.description: This week's TMW! ------------------------------------------------------------ Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d9698d02ad&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

Well. Having just had that milestone birthday (celebrated at different points through the weekend with my son and various friends), I think I am now officially a wizened political observer, and friends: I've never seen anything like this last week. The president of the United States posts “open the fuckin’ strait you crazy bastards” and threatens that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” You’d think that even Republicans in Congress would be a little uneasy at the prospect of a deranged dementia case igniting World War III, but I guess you would be mistaken. And until they start to turn on him, removal from office isn't going to happen, though I'm still in the "impeach him anyway" camp, because it's the right thing to do, and it's a matter of political messaging. (Not that Democratic leadership is interested in even raising the issue (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=6da3abbfbd&e=ed35377427) .) Anyway this cartoon is not about the

situation in Iran, exactly, and it’s definitely not meant as a 1:1 parallel. It’s about the overall anxiety, the constant sense of dread that is inescapable under Trump’s presidency. We're trapped on a roller coaster ride, and I hate roller coasters. “A whole civilization will die” is the most extreme example so far, but every morning, I wake up and check the news with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach, wondering what the absolute fuck this lunatic has done now. Or what he will do in a few hours. And then what he will do after that.

I myself am very tired of it! When I came up with this shirt (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=e1edf38171&e=ed35377427) thirteen or fourteen years ago, I thought it was just an amusing bit. I did not expect it to become my daily mantra.

I don’t actually enjoy writing cartoon after cartoon about Donald J. Trump, history’s most special boy. Those of you who were kind enough to back the Kickstarter, and have copies of OUR LONG NATIONAL NIGHTMARE (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=73bc1606bb&e=ed35377427) , can go back and look through — I had a much wider range of topics when Biden was president. Frankly, Biden rarely made appearances in the strip. I was okay with that. Though thinking back on it, that “wider range of topics” frequently included the possibility that Trump might regain power, and what a horrific prospect that was. And here we are!

And we’re only a year and change in. The only bright spot on the near horizon is the possibility of a blue wave in November, and while I am hoping for that as fervently as any of you, November feels like a long time away right now. A lot can happen between now and then, including the death of whole civilizations. I just don’t think the former host of Celebrity Apprentice should have that power.

NOTE TO IOWANS: my event in Iowa City is on Saturday (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2d6291cecf&e=ed35377427) , April 18 from 3-5 p.m. at Merge. It’s a panel with four other cartoonists, moderated by Kembrew McLeod. There will apparently be pizza! And even a limited number of copies of OLNN for sale, making this possibly the only opportunity for an in-person booksigning. And if you already have the book, or any of the others, feel feel to bring them, I’ll sign whatever you’ve got! I mean, within reason.

And on a completely different note, this is George. He is 28 years old and lives in the basement of an UWS pet store, down with the aquariums. I used to visit him more often when I had a fish tank, several years ago, and was strolling by on a pleasant afternoon and decided to drop in and see if he was still around. Happily, he is!

And finally, one for the "graphic design is my passion" file.

Wish list thanks this week to Karen C. And that's a wrap on another exhausting week.

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=11adb358ed&e=ed35377427)

The TT Tiny Store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=03c491344b&e=ed35377427) , featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.

Topataco store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=ba6506b395&e=ed35377427) -- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more

Threadless store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9e572f99f4&e=ed35377427) -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff

Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com

Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=aae8123aff&e=ed35377427)

Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=3774806e67&e=ed35377427)

Newsletter (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=0689b58ebd&e=ed35377427) (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)

Paypal donations (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=0ded26c9f1&e=ed35377427)

Tiny View link (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=0394a774fc&e=ed35377427) (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

============================================================

Email Marketing Powered by Mailchimp

** unsubscribe from this list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/unsubscribe?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&t=b&e=ed35377427&c=1399f506b6)

| ** update subscription preferences (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/profile?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&e=ed35377427&c=1399f506b6)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE UNITED STATES OF ANXIETY
item.description: This week's TMW! ------------------------------------------------------------ Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2df192466a&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

Well. Having just had that milestone birthday (celebrated at different points through the weekend with my son and various friends), I think I am now officially a wizened political observer, and friends: I've never seen anything like this last week. The president of the United States posts “open the fuckin’ strait you crazy bastards” and threatens that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” You’d think that even Republicans in Congress would be a little uneasy at the prospect of a deranged dementia case igniting World War III, but I guess you would be mistaken. And until they start to turn on him, removal from office isn't going to happen, though I'm still in the "impeach him anyway" camp, because it's the right thing to do, and it's a matter of political messaging. (Not that Democratic leadership is interested in even raising the issue (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=add9ff492c&e=ed35377427) .) Anyway this cartoon is not about the

situation in Iran, exactly, and it’s definitely not meant as a 1:1 parallel. It’s about the overall anxiety, the constant sense of dread that is inescapable under Trump’s presidency. We're trapped on a roller coaster ride, and I hate roller coasters. “A whole civilization will die” is the most extreme example so far, but every morning, I wake up and check the news with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach, wondering what the absolute fuck this lunatic has done now. Or what he will do in a few hours. And then what he will do after that.

I myself am very tired of it! When I came up with this shirt (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9a0d9b5a03&e=ed35377427) thirteen or fourteen years ago, I thought it was just an amusing bit. I did not expect it to become my daily mantra.

I don’t actually enjoy writing cartoon after cartoon about Donald J. Trump, history’s most special boy. Those of you who were kind enough to back the Kickstarter, and have copies of OUR LONG NATIONAL NIGHTMARE (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=edba161b2c&e=ed35377427) , can go back and look through — I had a much wider range of topics when Biden was president. Frankly, Biden rarely made appearances in the strip. I was okay with that. Though thinking back on it, that “wider range of topics” frequently included the possibility that Trump might regain power, and what a horrific prospect that was. And here we are!

And we’re only a year and change in. The only bright spot on the near horizon is the possibility of a blue wave in November, and while I am hoping for that as fervently as any of you, November feels like a long time away right now. A lot can happen between now and then, including the death of whole civilizations. I just don’t think the former host of Celebrity Apprentice should have that power.

NOTE TO IOWANS: my event in Iowa City is on Saturday (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d4fa835f2f&e=ed35377427) , April 18 from 3-5 p.m. at Merge. It’s a panel with four other cartoonists, moderated by Kembrew McLeod. There will apparently be pizza! And even a limited number of copies of OLNN for sale, making this possibly the only opportunity for an in-person booksigning. And if you already have the book, or any of the others, feel feel to bring them, I’ll sign whatever you’ve got! I mean, within reason.

And on a completely different note, this is George. He is 28 years old and lives in the basement of an UWS pet store, down with the aquariums. I used to visit him more often when I had a fish tank, several years ago, and was strolling by on a pleasant afternoon and decided to drop in and see if he was still around. Happily, he is!

And finally, one for the "graphic design is my passion" file.

Wish list thanks this week to Karen C. And that's a wrap on another exhausting week.

Until next time,

Dan

Wish list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9fb0128840&e=ed35377427)

The TT Tiny Store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2b9bdf0745&e=ed35377427) , featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.

Topataco store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=5ac6b7d4a5&e=ed35377427) -- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more

Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com

Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=df8f4c0caa&e=ed35377427)

Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=f4ade24a89&e=ed35377427)

Newsletter (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=5266f20686&e=ed35377427) (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)

Paypal donations (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d0138a356a&e=ed35377427)

Tiny View link (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=90e5b8092a&e=ed35377427) (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

============================================================

| ** update subscription preferences (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/profile?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&e=ed35377427&c=40686a8a56)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE INCREDIBLE SHRINKING TRUMP
item.description: This week's TMW! ------------------------------------------------------------ Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=16069e7db0&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

OK, this is admittedly a weird one. I was sitting at my desk trying to write a cartoon about How Donald Trump Fucks Up Everything, but it just wasn’t flowing, and felt flat and uninspired, and I just wanted to have some fun. I had a random thought about a doing a 50 Foot Woman bit, with Trump growing oversized and out of control, but then I got to thinking that actually it’s the opposite, we’re watching him grow diminished in real time, with approval ratings at historic lows even compared to his first term. Now, I have only the vaguest memory of The Incredible Shrinking Man, which I guess I probably saw as a kid at some point, so I took a quick look at the summary, and saw that the character starts shrinking after being exposed to a “mysterious cloud of gas.” I started turning that over in my head, and it occurred to me that Elon Musk is constantly being fined for dumping toxic waste from his various enterprises, so I thought that would be an appropriate way to start the ball rolling.

Panel two sets up the premise (obvious from the title of the strip of course). The third panel was a no-brainer — faced with a medical mystery like this, who does Trump have on hand to call? The experts he has put in charge of the nation’s health care system, obviously! The fourth and fifth panels just came to me unbidden — Trump in front of a giant phone, texting with both hands (note the bruise), characteristically denying that there are any problems whatsoever, and then getting swept off the desk by an errant feather duster. (Trump actually did misspell “strait” in a post, which put me in an awkward position — if I spelled it correctly, readers would be reminding me of the misspelling all week, but if I misspelled it, other readers would think the mistake was mine. I decided to thread the needle by putting the word in Trumpian quotes.) Then the question was, how to finish up? I could have gone with his ultimate fate — and if there were a White House cat, that would have clearly been the

way to end it, per the classic movie still of the tiny guy being chased by a giant cat, but of course everyone knows Trump hates pets, so that wasn’t gonna work. And then my mind wandered back to the original 50 Foot Woman riff, and I decided to close with that, using Miller to give the cartoon a bit of an ominous ending — even though we will eventually be done with Trump, the fascist, authoritarian movement he has empowered will still be with us. It also seemed appropriate to portray Miller as a vengeful lunatic with far too much power.

There are a lot of other panels I could have included in this, with so many potential scenarios, such as the reaction of right wing media to the shrinking president. I wrote one panel that didn’t make it in with Jesse Watters saying, “We must stand behind our tiny president — but be careful not to step ON him!” Which is a pretty good line, but there just wasn’t quite enough room.

Last week’s cartoon, with shirtless RFK eating fried rats, Trump in Sherlockian mode, “Drinky Guy” Hegseth and Bellhop Vance, still somehow managed to inspire a couple of “this isn’t satire, it’s just what’s really happening” comments, which long time readers may remember is my absolute least-favorite genre of feedback, as it both cheapens the effort I put into these things and absolutely misunderstands the nature of satire. If someone looks at *this* week’s cartoon and is inspired to post that comment, I think my head may literally explode. (Fortunately I’m starting up on Medicare on April 1!)

And finally, a few quick shots from Saturday's No Kings march, starting off with Padma Lakshi, Deniro and Sharpton at the front of the march:

And this was fun -- people send me pics all the time but I rarely run across my own work in the wild at these things. The Trump face on the sign is a mask IDW gave out at New York Comic Con in 2016, before he'd even been elected. Because it was such an early caricature, I had to look at it twice to be certain it was actually my own work!

Okay. Wish list thanks this week to David B., Barbara H., and (as often happens) an unintentionally anonymous gifter. Speaking of, several readers have alerted me to the changes in Amazon’s policy which might make it possible for my home address to be revealed, which of course I’m cautious about. But having dug into it a bit, it seems that this only applies to third-party sellers, so I’ll just be avoiding those.

And, one more reminder -- we currently have +/- 70 unsigned copies of OLNN remaining, and about 30 signed. If you want one, or want another one to give to someone, act quickly, (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=90ed3d1ae3&e=ed35377427) because we are close to clearing these things out.

Until next week!

Wish list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=69c9998bd2&e=ed35377427)

The TT Tiny Store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=4598dfd515&e=ed35377427) , featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.

Topataco store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=b9de3229d5&e=ed35377427) -- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more

Threadless store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=3324884ae3&e=ed35377427) -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff

Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com

Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=cb548a5f9e&e=ed35377427)

Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=e0357049c2&e=ed35377427)

Newsletter (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1208f93092&e=ed35377427) (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)

Paypal donations (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=012af47977&e=ed35377427)

Tiny View link (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=baed7aa1b6&e=ed35377427) (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

============================================================

Email Marketing Powered by Mailchimp

** unsubscribe from this list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/unsubscribe?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&t=b&e=ed35377427&c=a3fb266e8f)

| ** update subscription preferences (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/profile?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&e=ed35377427&c=a3fb266e8f)

item.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] DETECTIVE-IN-CHIEF
item.description: This week's TMW! ------------------------------------------------------------ Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky" READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your…
item.author: Tom Tomorrow
item.content:encoded:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1cf669bb1e&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

My challenge each week, or at least most weeks, is figuring out how to encapsulate the ongoing, omnidirectional insanity and chaos of this administration. RFK Jr. doesn’t have anything to do with the main theme of this cartoon, but I wanted to include him as a reminder that there’s always something else going on at the same time, and it’s exhausting. As Trump and Hegseth celebrate Schroedinger’s war, which we have already won and yet are continuing to escalate, RFK is still working to tank the nation’s health infrastructure, Stephen Miller is still plotting the expulsion of everyone he hates, apparently Tom Homan is spending the day planning to deploy ICE to airports tomorrow, etc., etc. Also RFK released a weird a.i. video last week showing himself shirtless, fighting a (computelr generated) guy in a Twinkie suit in a wrestling ring, and I just thought it would be funny if shirtless RFK became a recurrent theme, like bellhop Vance. Walz had it exactly right in 2024 — these people are just

*weird*.

Newt Gingrich really did suggest nuking a new opening as an alternative to the Strait of Hormuz. And I wanted to at least gesture toward the fact that Vance is walking a tightrope, forced to support his degenerate master’s pointless war, but trying to signal that he’s not fully on board, because he knows full well that this is all going to go sideways in a big hurry and he still dreams of being president some day. And of course, Hegseth did say, out loud, that he can’t wait until Ellison takes over CNN so news coverage will be more favorable. Which is exactly how Putin handled the media in the early aughts; the second wave of oligarchs were Putin allies, and they took over Russian news media to ensure favorable coverage.

I set this one on the White House lawn just for a change of pace, visually, from all the Oval Office cartoons I do.

It is a strange moment, in which we stand on the brink of an abyss. I mean, yes, it feels like we’ve been standing on the brink of an abyss for quite a long time now, but now we’re facing a global oil shock, a global economic meltdown, even global famine as fertilizer supplies have been interrupted by the Hormuz closure. (I owe some taxes this year, due to the Kickstarter, but at least I can rest secure in the knowledge that my money will be spent on a pointless war that’s going to make everything more expensive!) And those are just the ancillary complications of this idiotic war of choice, which could absolutely escalate at a moment’s notice. Meanwhile, Donald Trump obsesses over his ballroom, and having the mint produce a gold coin with his face on it. And he also wants his birthday made into a federal holiday, his face on Mount Rushmore (that one’s old news, but apparently still an active obsession), and a “Trump-shaped monument built in our nation’s capital.

(https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=c201ad6f94&e=ed35377427) ” He may set some sort of record for the inverse relationship between his tanking approval and the number of monuments to himself he leaves behind. It all reminds me of this cartoon from 2017:

I’ve got some wish list thanks that will need to wait until next week. Also, it seems that it might be time to wind down the wish list, as Amazon has made some changes which could potentially expose my home address. I’m keeping an eye on that.

Finally, since it has been awhile, here's a bonus picture of the indolent gentleman of leisure with whom I share my apartment.

Until next week!

Dan

Wish list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=a121f1ea75&e=ed35377427)

The TT Tiny Store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=ed9f755085&e=ed35377427) , featuring remaining print and ebook copies of Our Long National Nightmare, as well as the ebook for 25 Years of Tomorrow.

Topataco store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1f246dfdd9&e=ed35377427) -- t-shirts, signed prints, plush Sparkies, and more

Threadless store (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=7710527f2e&e=ed35377427) -- more ephemeral designs, as well as prints, coffee mugs, other print-on-demand stuff

Personal website (mostly inactive): www.thismodernworld.com

Instagram (mostly inactive): @realtomtomorrow (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1cfb7b1206&e=ed35377427)

Bluesky (excessively active): @tomtomorrow.bsky.social (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9aeb4420d2&e=ed35377427)

Newsletter (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=4586a337e2&e=ed35377427) (for those of you enjoying a forwarded copy — please remember that this newsletter is intended for paid subscribers who keep my work afloat! Please consider getting your own subscription!!)

Paypal donations (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9840b8cd59&e=ed35377427)

Tiny View link (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=33f43baedd&e=ed35377427) (PLEASE CLICK ON THE NEWEST CARTOON! my pay scales up based on page views!)

============================================================

Email Marketing Powered by Mailchimp

** unsubscribe from this list (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/unsubscribe?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&t=b&e=ed35377427&c=a964547bef)

| ** update subscription preferences (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/profile?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9903d575d3&e=ed35377427&c=a964547bef)
